docs.title: kmeanssa-ng
docs.text:
K-means clustering on quantum graphs and metric spaces using simulated annealing

kmeanssa-ng is a modern Python package for solving the k-means problem on arbitrary metric spaces, with a focus on quantum graphs — metric graphs where points can lie anywhere on edges, not just at nodes.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
What makes kmeanssa-ng special?

  • 🌐 Quantum Graph Support: Full clustering on quantum graphs with points anywhere on edges
  • 🔥 Simulated Annealing: Robust k-means combining Brownian motion and drift
  • 🎯 Flexible Architecture: Extensible to any metric space via abstract base classes
  • ⚡ Smart Initialization: k-means++ initialization for faster convergence
  • 🛡️ Robustification: Averaging of final iterations for stable results
  • 🏗️ Graph Generators: Pre-built generators for testing and research
  • 📝 Type Hints: Fully typed codebase with modern Python features

docs.title: Algorithm Overview
docs.text:
The core algorithm alternates between two phases:

graph LR
    A[Initialize Centers] --> B[Brownian Motion]
    B --> C[Drift Phase]
    C --> D{Converged?}
    D -->|No| B
    D -->|Yes| E[Robustification]
    E --> F[Final Centers]
  1. Brownian Motion (exploration): Centers perform random walks on the metric space
  2. Drift (exploitation): Centers move toward their nearest observations
  3. Robustification: Averages results from final iterations for stability

Temperature is controlled by an inhomogeneous Poisson process for optimal convergence.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, SimulatedAnnealing

# Create a quantum graph with 2 clusters
graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[50, 50],
    p=[[0.7, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7]]
)
graph.precomputing()

# Sample points and run clustering
points = graph.sample_points(100)
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, lambda_param=1, beta=1.0)
centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The package follows a clean three-layer architecture:

graph TB
    subgraph "Implementation Layer"
        QG[QuantumGraph]
        QGP[QGPoint/QGCenter]
        QGSA[QGSimulatedAnnealing]
    end

    subgraph "Algorithm Layer"
        SA[SimulatedAnnealing]
    end

    subgraph "Abstract Layer"
        ABS[Point/Center/Space]
    end

    QG --> SA
    QGP --> ABS
    QGSA --> SA
    SA --> ABS

This design allows easy extension to new metric spaces while maintaining algorithmic consistency.

docs.title: Hierarchical k-means with Different Scales
docs.text:
from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, SimulatedAnnealing, QGSimulatedAnnealing
import numpy as np
import networkx as nx

# Create a complex multi-scale graph
complex_graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[50, 50, 40, 40, 30, 30],  # 6 blocks of varying sizes
    p=[[0.9, 0.7, 0.1, 0.1, 0.05, 0.05],   # Block 1: connected to 2
       [0.7, 0.9, 0.1, 0.1, 0.05, 0.05],   # Block 2: connected to 1
       [0.1, 0.1, 0.8, 0.6, 0.1, 0.05],    # Block 3: connected to 4
       [0.1, 0.1, 0.6, 0.8, 0.1, 0.05],    # Block 4: connected to 3
       [0.05, 0.05, 0.1, 0.1, 0.9, 0.7],   # Block 5: connected to 6
       [0.05, 0.05, 0.05, 0.05, 0.7, 0.9]] # Block 6: connected to 5
)

points_complex = complex_graph.sample_points(300)
print(f"Generated complex graph: {len(complex_graph.nodes())} nodes, {len(complex_graph.edges())} edges")
print(f"Sampled {len(points_complex)} points for hierarchical clustering")

# Simplified cluster refinement analysis
def analyze_cluster_hierarchy(hierarchical_results, block_assignments):
    """Analyze hierarchical clustering results."""

    print("Hierarchical Clustering Analysis:")

    # Compare cluster counts with true block structure
    n_true_blocks = len(set(block_assignments.values()))
    print(f"True number of blocks in graph: {n_true_blocks}")

    for k in sorted(hierarchical_results.keys()):
        result = hierarchical_results[k]
        print(f"k={k}: Energy={result['energy']:.3f}, Node clusters={result['node_clusters']}")

        # Count unique clusters
        unique_clusters = len(set(result['node_clusters']))
        print(f"  Actual unique clusters found: {unique_clusters}")

        if k == n_true_blocks:
            print(f"  ↳ Matches true block count!")

    print(f"\nEnergy decreases with more clusters (expected):")
    energies = [hierarchical_results[k]['energy'] for k in sorted(hierarchical_results.keys())]
    decreasing = all(energies[i] >= energies[i+1] for i in range(len(energies)-1))
    print(f"  Energy sequence: {[f'{e:.3f}' for e in energies]}")
    print(f"  Monotonically decreasing: {decreasing}")

docs.title: Grid Search for Optimal Parameters
docs.text:
from itertools import product
import time

# Create a standard test case
test_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[40, 40, 40], p=[[0.8, 0.1, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 0.8]])
test_points = test_graph.sample_points(150)

# Define parameter grid
param_grid = {
    'lambda_param': [0.5, 1.0, 2.0],
    'beta': [0.5, 1.0, 2.0],
    'step_size': [0.05, 0.1, 0.2],
    'robust_prop': [0.05, 0.1, 0.15]
}

print(f"Testing {np.prod([len(v) for v in param_grid.values()])} parameter combinations...")

# Grid search with performance tracking
best_params = None
best_energy = float('inf')
results = []

# Sample subset for faster testing
param_combinations = list(product(*param_grid.values()))[:15]  # Test first 15 combinations

for i, (lambda_p, beta_p, step_p, robust_p) in enumerate(param_combinations):
    start_time = time.time()

    try:
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(
            test_points, k=3,
            lambda_param=lambda_p,
            beta=beta_p,
            step_size=step_p
        )

        centers = sa.run(robust_prop=robust_p, initialization="kpp")
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, test_points)
        runtime = time.time() - start_time

        results.append({
            'lambda': lambda_p,
            'beta': beta_p,
            'step_size': step_p,
            'robust_prop': robust_p,
            'energy': energy,
            'runtime': runtime
        })

        if energy < best_energy:
            best_energy = energy
            best_params = (lambda_p, beta_p, step_p, robust_p)

        if i % 5 == 0:
            print(f"Completed {i+1}/{len(param_combinations)}: Best energy so far: {best_energy:.3f}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Failed params {(lambda_p, beta_p, step_p, robust_p)}: {e}")

print(f"\nBest parameters: λ={best_params[0]}, β={best_params[1]}, h={best_params[2]}, robust={best_params[3]}")
print(f"Best energy: {best_energy:.3f}")

docs.title: Adaptive Parameter Selection
docs.text:
def adaptive_parameter_selection(points, k, max_iterations=10):
    """Adaptively select parameters based on graph properties."""

    # Start with default parameters
    current_params = {'lambda_param': 1.0, 'beta': 1.0, 'step_size': 0.1}
    current_energy = float('inf')

    # Graph analysis for parameter hints
    n_points = len(points)
    graph = points[0].space

    # Estimate graph density and complexity
    n_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    n_edges = len(graph.edges())
    density = 2 * n_edges / (n_nodes * (n_nodes - 1)) if n_nodes > 1 else 0

    print(f"Graph analysis: {n_nodes} nodes, density={density:.3f}")

    # Adaptive rules
    if density > 0.1:  # Dense graph
        current_params['lambda_param'] = 2.0  # Higher exploration
        current_params['step_size'] = 0.05    # Smaller steps
        print("Dense graph detected → increased exploration, smaller steps")
    elif density < 0.05:  # Sparse graph
        current_params['beta'] = 2.0          # Faster convergence
        current_params['step_size'] = 0.15    # Larger steps
        print("Sparse graph detected → faster convergence, larger steps")

    if n_points > 200:  # Many points
        current_params['lambda_param'] *= 1.5
        print(f"Many points ({n_points}) → increased exploration")

    # Test adaptive parameters
    sa_adaptive = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k, **current_params)
    centers_adaptive = sa_adaptive.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")
    adaptive_energy = sa_adaptive.calculate_energy(centers_adaptive, points)

    print(f"Adaptive parameters: {current_params}")
    print(f"Adaptive energy: {adaptive_energy:.3f}")

    return centers_adaptive, current_params

# Test adaptive selection
adaptive_centers, adaptive_params = adaptive_parameter_selection(test_points, k=3)
adaptive_energy = SimulatedAnnealing(test_points, k=3, **adaptive_params).calculate_energy(adaptive_centers, test_points)

# Compare with default parameters
sa_default = SimulatedAnnealing(test_points, k=3)  # All defaults
default_centers = sa_default.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")
default_energy = sa_default.calculate_energy(default_centers, test_points)

print(f"\nComparison:")
print(f"Default parameters energy: {default_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Adaptive parameters energy: {adaptive_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Improvement: {default_energy - adaptive_energy:.3f}")

docs.title: Graph Cleaning Techniques
docs.text:
def clean_graph_by_centrality(graph, threshold=0.1):
    """Remove edges with low centrality to reduce noise."""
    # Calculate edge betweenness centrality
    edge_centrality = nx.edge_betweenness_centrality(graph, weight='length')

    # Identify edges to remove
    edges_to_remove = [
        edge for edge, centrality in edge_centrality.items() 
        if centrality < threshold
    ]

    print(f"Removing {len(edges_to_remove)} edges with centrality < {threshold}")

    # Create cleaned graph
    cleaned_graph = graph.copy()
    cleaned_graph.remove_edges_from(edges_to_remove)

    # Ensure connectivity
    if not nx.is_connected(cleaned_graph):
        # Work with largest component
        largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(cleaned_graph), key=len)
        cleaned_graph = cleaned_graph.subgraph(largest_cc).copy()
        print(f"Keeping largest component: {len(cleaned_graph.nodes())} nodes")

    return cleaned_graph

# Apply cleaning with less aggressive threshold
cleaned_graph = clean_graph_by_centrality(noisy_graph, threshold=0.01)

if len(cleaned_graph.nodes()) > 10 and len(cleaned_graph.edges()) > 5:
    cleaned_graph.precomputing()
    print(f"Cleaned graph: {len(cleaned_graph.nodes())} nodes, {len(cleaned_graph.edges())} edges")

    # Compare clustering quality
    comparison_data = []
    graphs_to_test = [("Noisy", noisy_graph), ("Cleaned", cleaned_graph)]

    for name, graph in graphs_to_test:
        points = graph.sample_points(80)  # Reduce sample size
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)
        centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
        comparison_data.append((name, energy))
        print(f"{name} graph clustering energy: {energy:.3f}")

    print(f"Cleaning improvement: {comparison_data[0][1] - comparison_data[1][1]:.3f}")
else:
    print(f"Cleaning was too aggressive - cleaned graph has only {len(cleaned_graph.nodes())} nodes")
    print("Skipping comparison with overly-cleaned graph")

docs.title: Graph Structure Analysis
docs.text:
def analyze_graph_structure(graph, name):
    """Analyze basic structural properties of the graph."""

    print(f"=== {name} Graph Structure ===")

    # Basic statistics
    n_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    n_edges = len(graph.edges())
    density = 2 * n_edges / (n_nodes * (n_nodes - 1)) if n_nodes > 1 else 0

    print(f"Nodes: {n_nodes}")
    print(f"Edges: {n_edges}")
    print(f"Density: {density:.3f}")

    # Clustering coefficient
    clustering_coeff = nx.average_clustering(graph, weight='length')
    print(f"Average clustering coefficient: {clustering_coeff:.3f}")

    # Degree distribution
    degrees = [graph.degree(n) for n in graph.nodes()]
    avg_degree = np.mean(degrees)
    max_degree = max(degrees)
    min_degree = min(degrees)

    print(f"Degree: avg={avg_degree:.1f}, min={min_degree}, max={max_degree}")

    # Connected components
    n_components = nx.number_connected_components(graph)
    if n_components == 1:
        print("Graph is connected")
        # Diameter (only for connected graphs)
        try:
            diameter = nx.diameter(graph, weight='length')
            print(f"Diameter: {diameter:.3f}")
        except:
            print("Could not compute diameter")
    else:
        print(f"Graph has {n_components} connected components")

    print()

# Analyze both graph structures
analyze_graph_structure(noisy_graph, "Noisy")
if len(cleaned_graph.nodes()) > 1:  # Only if cleaning didn't destroy the graph
    analyze_graph_structure(cleaned_graph, "Cleaned")

docs.title: Clustering with Size Constraints
docs.text:
def constrained_clustering(points, k, min_cluster_size=None, max_cluster_size=None):
    """Clustering with cluster size constraints."""

    if min_cluster_size is None:
        min_cluster_size = len(points) // (k * 3)  # At least 1/3 of average
    if max_cluster_size is None:
        max_cluster_size = len(points) // k * 2    # At most 2x average

    print(f"Constrained clustering: {min_cluster_size} ≤ cluster size ≤ {max_cluster_size}")

    best_centers = None
    best_score = float('inf')
    attempts = 0
    max_attempts = 20

    while attempts < max_attempts:
        # Run standard clustering
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
        centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.05)

        # Assign points to clusters
        cluster_assignments = []
        cluster_sizes = [0] * k

        for point in points:
            distances = [point.space.distance(point, center) for center in centers]
            closest_cluster = np.argmin(distances)
            cluster_assignments.append(closest_cluster)
            cluster_sizes[closest_cluster] += 1

        # Check size constraints
        valid_clustering = all(
            min_cluster_size <= size <= max_cluster_size 
            for size in cluster_sizes
        )

        if valid_clustering:
            energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
            if energy < best_score:
                best_score = energy
                best_centers = centers
                print(f"Found valid clustering: energy={energy:.3f}, sizes={cluster_sizes}")
                break

        attempts += 1

    if best_centers is None:
        print(f"Warning: Could not find valid clustering in {max_attempts} attempts")
        # Fallback to unconstrained
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
        best_centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)

    return best_centers

# Test constrained clustering
test_points_constrained = test_graph.sample_points(120)
constrained_centers = constrained_clustering(test_points_constrained, k=3, min_cluster_size=30, max_cluster_size=50)

# Verify final cluster sizes
final_assignments = []
for point in test_points_constrained:
    distances = [point.space.distance(point, center) for center in constrained_centers]
    final_assignments.append(np.argmin(distances))

final_sizes = [final_assignments.count(i) for i in range(3)]
print(f"Final cluster sizes: {final_sizes}")

docs.title: Multi-Objective Optimization
docs.text:
def multi_objective_clustering(points, k, objectives=['energy', 'silhouette', 'separation']):
    """Multi-objective clustering optimization."""

    def calculate_silhouette_score(points, centers):
        """Simple silhouette score approximation."""
        if len(centers) < 2:
            return 0

        scores = []
        for point in points[:20]:  # Sample for speed
            distances = [point.space.distance(point, center) for center in centers]
            closest_idx = np.argmin(distances)

            # Average distance to own cluster center (approximation)
            a = distances[closest_idx]

            # Average distance to nearest other cluster center
            other_distances = [distances[i] for i in range(len(centers)) if i != closest_idx]
            b = min(other_distances) if other_distances else a

            if b > a:
                scores.append((b - a) / b)
            else:
                scores.append(0)

        return np.mean(scores) if scores else 0

    def calculate_separation(centers):
        """Average separation between cluster centers."""
        if len(centers) < 2:
            return 0

        separations = []
        for i, center1 in enumerate(centers):
            for center2 in centers[i+1:]:
                separations.append(center1.space.distance(center1, center2))

        return np.mean(separations)

    # Multiple runs with different trade-offs
    results = []
    weights_sets = [
        {'energy': 1.0, 'silhouette': 0.0, 'separation': 0.0},    # Pure energy
        {'energy': 0.7, 'silhouette': 0.3, 'separation': 0.0},   # Energy + silhouette
        {'energy': 0.5, 'silhouette': 0.3, 'separation': 0.2},   # Balanced
        {'energy': 0.4, 'silhouette': 0.4, 'separation': 0.2},   # Silhouette focused
    ]

    for i, weights in enumerate(weights_sets):
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
        centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)

        # Calculate all objectives
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
        silhouette = calculate_silhouette_score(points, centers)
        separation = calculate_separation(centers)

        # Normalize and combine (simplified)
        normalized_energy = energy / len(points)  # Rough normalization
        combined_score = (weights['energy'] * normalized_energy - 
                         weights['silhouette'] * silhouette + 
                         weights['separation'] / separation if separation > 0 else 0)

        results.append({
            'weights': weights,
            'centers': centers,
            'energy': energy,
            'silhouette': silhouette,
            'separation': separation,
            'combined_score': combined_score
        })

        print(f"Config {i+1}: Energy={energy:.3f}, Silhouette={silhouette:.3f}, Separation={separation:.3f}")

    # Find best according to combined score
    best_result = min(results, key=lambda x: x['combined_score'])
    print(f"\nBest multi-objective solution:")
    print(f"Weights: {best_result['weights']}")
    print(f"Energy: {best_result['energy']:.3f}")
    print(f"Silhouette: {best_result['silhouette']:.3f}")
    print(f"Separation: {best_result['separation']:.3f}")

    return best_result['centers']

# Test multi-objective optimization
multi_obj_centers = multi_objective_clustering(test_points[:80], k=3)  # Smaller subset for speed

def analyze_clustering_stability(points, k, n_runs=10):
    """Analyze stability of clustering across multiple runs."""

    all_energies = []
    all_center_positions = []

    print(f"Analyzing clustering stability over {n_runs} runs...")

    for run in range(n_runs):
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
        centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)

        all_energies.append(energy)

        # Extract center positions (for quantum graphs, get representative positions)
        if hasattr(centers[0], 'edge') and hasattr(centers[0], 'position'):
            center_positions = [(c.edge, c.position) for c in centers]
        else:
            center_positions = [str(c) for c in centers]  # Fallback representation

        all_center_positions.append(center_positions)

        if run % 3 == 0:
            print(f"Run {run+1}/{n_runs}: Energy = {energy:.3f}")

    # Stability analysis
    energy_mean = np.mean(all_energies)
    energy_std = np.std(all_energies)
    energy_cv = energy_std / energy_mean if energy_mean > 0 else 0

    print(f"\nStability Analysis:")
    print(f"Energy: μ={energy_mean:.3f}, σ={energy_std:.3f}, CV={energy_cv:.3f}")
    print(f"Best energy: {min(all_energies):.3f}")
    print(f"Worst energy: {max(all_energies):.3f}")

    # Stability quality assessment
    if energy_cv < 0.05:
        stability = "Excellent"
    elif energy_cv < 0.10:
        stability = "Good"  
    elif energy_cv < 0.20:
        stability = "Fair"
    else:
        stability = "Poor"

    print(f"Stability assessment: {stability}")

    return {
        'energies': all_energies,
        'mean_energy': energy_mean,
        'std_energy': energy_std,
        'stability': stability
    }

# Analyze stability for different configurations
stability_results = {}

configs = [
    ('Standard', {'k': 3, 'graph': test_graph}),
    ('High k', {'k': 6, 'graph': test_graph}),
    ('Complex graph', {'k': 3, 'graph': complex_graph})
]

for name, config in configs:
    print(f"\n=== {name} Configuration ===")
    test_pts = config['graph'].sample_points(100)
    stability_results[name] = analyze_clustering_stability(test_pts, config['k'], n_runs=8)

docs.title: Performance Profiling
docs.text:
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def timer(description):
    """Context manager for timing operations."""
    start = time.time()
    yield
    end = time.time()
    print(f"{description}: {end - start:.3f} seconds")

def performance_profile(points, k_values=[2, 3, 4, 5]):
    """Profile performance across different k values and parameters."""

    print(f"Performance profiling with {len(points)} points...")

    results = {}

    for k in k_values:
        print(f"\n--- k = {k} ---")

        # Test different algorithms and parameters
        configs = [
            ('Standard v1', {'algorithm_version': 'v1', 'robust_prop': 0.1}),
            ('Standard v2', {'algorithm_version': 'v2', 'robust_prop': 0.1}),
            ('Fast', {'robust_prop': 0.05, 'beta': 2.0, 'step_size': 0.2}),
            ('Precise', {'robust_prop': 0.15, 'beta': 0.5, 'step_size': 0.05}),
        ]

        k_results = {}

        for name, params in configs:
            with timer(f"{name:>12}"):
                sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k, **{p: v for p, v in params.items() 
                                                       if p not in ['algorithm_version', 'robust_prop']})
                algorithm_version = params.get('algorithm_version', 'v1')
                robust_prop = params.get('robust_prop', 0.1)

                centers = sa.run(
                    algorithm_version=algorithm_version,
                    robust_prop=robust_prop,
                    initialization="kpp"
                )
                energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)

            k_results[name] = {'energy': energy, 'centers': centers}
            print(f"             Energy: {energy:.3f}")

        results[k] = k_results

    # Summary of best configurations
    print(f"\n=== Performance Summary ===")
    for k, k_results in results.items():
        best_config = min(k_results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['energy'])
        print(f"k={k}: Best = {best_config[0]} (energy={best_config[1]['energy']:.3f})")

    return results

# Run performance profiling
if len(test_points) >= 60:  # Only if we have enough points
    perf_results = performance_profile(test_points[:80], k_values=[2, 3, 4])
else:
    print("Skipping performance profiling - need more points")

docs.text:
def export_clustering_results(centers, points, filename_base="clustering_results"):
    """Export clustering results in multiple formats."""

    # Assign points to clusters
    assignments = []
    for point in points:
        distances = [point.space.distance(point, center) for center in centers]
        assignments.append(np.argmin(distances))

    # Create results dictionary
    results = {
        'n_points': len(points),
        'n_clusters': len(centers),
        'cluster_assignments': assignments,
        'cluster_sizes': [assignments.count(i) for i in range(len(centers))]
    }

    # Export basic statistics
    print(f"Clustering Results Export:")
    print(f"Points: {results['n_points']}")
    print(f"Clusters: {results['n_clusters']}")
    print(f"Cluster sizes: {results['cluster_sizes']}")

    # Calculate clustering quality metrics
    total_energy = sum(
        min(point.space.distance(point, center)**2 for center in centers)
        for point in points
    )
    avg_energy = total_energy / len(points)

    results['total_energy'] = total_energy
    results['average_energy'] = avg_energy

    print(f"Average energy per point: {avg_energy:.3f}")

    return results

def compare_with_baseline(points, k, baseline_method="random"):
    """Compare clustering results with baseline methods."""

    # Our method
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
    our_centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
    our_energy = sa.calculate_energy(our_centers, points)

    # Baseline methods
    baseline_results = {}

    if baseline_method == "random":
        # Random clustering
        random_centers = points[0].space.sample_centers(k)
        random_energy = sa.calculate_energy(random_centers, points)
        baseline_results['random'] = random_energy

    # K-means++ initialization only (no optimization)
    kpp_centers = points[0].space.sample_kpp_centers(k)
    kpp_energy = sa.calculate_energy(kpp_centers, points)
    baseline_results['kpp_only'] = kpp_energy

    print(f"Method Comparison (lower is better):")
    print(f"Our SA method:     {our_energy:.3f}")
    for method, energy in baseline_results.items():
        improvement = energy - our_energy
        improvement_pct = (improvement / energy) * 100 if energy > 0 else 0
        print(f"{method:>15}: {energy:.3f} (improvement: {improvement:+.3f}, {improvement_pct:.1f}%)")

    return our_centers, baseline_results

# Export results and compare methods with simple test case
if len(test_points) >= 40:
    # Use test_points that match the test_graph
    simple_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(test_points[:60], k=3)
    simple_centers = simple_sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)

    export_results = export_clustering_results(simple_centers, test_points[:60])
    final_centers, baselines = compare_with_baseline(test_points[:60], k=3)

docs.title: Basic Clustering Examples
docs.text: This guide provides practical examples of using kmeanssa-ng for various clustering scenarios.

docs.title: Example 1: Two-Cluster Stochastic Block Model
docs.text:
The most basic scenario - clustering points on a graph with clear community structure:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, QGSimulatedAnnealing, SimulatedAnnealing

# Generate a graph with two distinct communities
graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[40, 40],       # Two communities of 40 nodes each
    p=[[0.8, 0.1],        # High intra-community connectivity  
       [0.1, 0.8]],       # Low inter-community connectivity
)

# Essential: precompute shortest paths
graph.precomputing()

# Sample points uniformly across the graph
points = graph.sample_points(150)

print(f"Created graph with {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes and {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
print(f"Sampled {len(points)} points on the graph")

Created graph with 80 nodes and 1377 edges
Sampled 150 points on the graph

# Run quantum graph specialized simulated annealing
qg_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(
    observations=points,
    k=2,                  # We know there are 2 clusters
    lambda_param=1.0,     # Standard temperature
    beta=1.0,            # Standard drift strength
    step_size=0.1        # Standard step size
)

# Get cluster centers as node IDs (more interpretable)
node_centers = qg_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Cluster centers near nodes: {node_centers}")

# For comparison, exact positions with base class
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)
centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")
print(f"Found {len(centers)} exact centers")

Cluster centers near nodes: [44, 12]
Found 2 exact centers

# Display results
print("\nQuantum Graph specific results:")
for i, node_id in enumerate(node_centers):
    print(f"  Cluster {i+1} center near node: {node_id}")

print("\nExact center positions:")
for i, center in enumerate(centers[:2]):  # Show first 2
    print(f"  Cluster {i+1}: {center}")

docs.title: Example 3: Using Existing NetworkX Graphs
docs.text:
Convert real-world networks to quantum graphs:

import networkx as nx
from kmeanssa_ng import as_quantum_graph

# Start with a well-known network
G = nx.karate_club_graph()

# Convert to quantum graph with uniform edge lengths
karate_graph = as_quantum_graph(G, edge_length=1.0)
karate_graph.precomputing()

print(f"Karate club graph: {karate_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {karate_graph.number_of_edges()} edges")

Karate club graph: 34 nodes, 78 edges

# Sample points and cluster
karate_points = karate_graph.sample_points(50)
qg_sa_karate = QGSimulatedAnnealing(karate_points, k=2)  # Karate club has 2 known communities
karate_nodes = qg_sa_karate.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

print(f"Karate club clustering centers near nodes: {karate_nodes}")

Karate club clustering centers near nodes: [0, 33]

# You can also use custom edge lengths based on graph properties
G_weighted = nx.karate_club_graph()

# Add custom weights (e.g., inverse of edge betweenness)
betweenness = nx.edge_betweenness_centrality(G_weighted)
for (u, v), centrality in betweenness.items():
    G_weighted[u][v]['length'] = 1.0 / (centrality + 0.01)  # Avoid division by zero

# Convert to quantum graph first, then update lengths
karate_weighted = as_quantum_graph(G_weighted, edge_length=1.0)  # Default lengths
# Update with custom lengths
for (u, v), centrality in betweenness.items():
    karate_weighted[u][v]['length'] = 1.0 / (centrality + 0.01)

karate_weighted.precomputing()
print(f"Weighted karate club graph created successfully")

Weighted karate club graph created successfully

import numpy as np

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Understanding the fundamental concepts behind kmeanssa-ng will help you use the library effectively and extend it for your own research.

docs.title: What are Quantum Graphs?
docs.text:
A quantum graph is a metric graph where:

  • Nodes are connected by edges with positive lengths
  • Points can exist anywhere on edges, not just at nodes
  • Distance is measured along the graph (geodesic distance)
from kmeanssa_ng import QuantumGraph, QGPoint

# Create a simple triangle graph
graph = QuantumGraph()
graph.add_edge(0, 1, length=1.0)
graph.add_edge(1, 2, length=1.5) 
graph.add_edge(2, 0, length=2.0)

print(f"Triangle graph: {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")

Triangle graph: 3 nodes, 3 edges

# Points can be anywhere on edges
point_a = QGPoint(graph, edge=(0, 1), position=0.3)  # 30% along edge (0,1)
point_b = QGPoint(graph, edge=(1, 2), position=0.8)  # 80% along edge (1,2)

print(f"Point A: {point_a}")
print(f"Point B: {point_b}")

# Distance is measured along the graph
graph.precomputing()  # Essential for distance calculations
distance = graph.distance(point_a, point_b)
print(f"Distance between points: {distance:.3f}")

Point A: QGPoint on edge (0, 1) at position 0.300
Point B: QGPoint on edge (1, 2) at position 0.800
Distance between points: 1.500

docs.title: Why Quantum Graphs?
docs.text:
Traditional clustering algorithms assume points live in Euclidean spaces (like 2D or 3D). But many real-world scenarios involve:

  • Road networks - GPS coordinates along roads
  • Neural networks - Signals propagating along connections
  • Social networks - Information spreading through connections
  • River networks - Pollution or species distribution along waterways

docs.title: Simulated Annealing for K-means
docs.text: The core algorithm combines two processes:

docs.title: 1. Brownian Motion (Exploration)
docs.text:
Centers perform random walks to explore the space:

Purpose: Avoid getting stuck in local minima by exploring widely.

docs.title: 2. Drift (Exploitation)
docs.text:
Centers move toward their nearest data points:

Purpose: Refine center positions based on actual data.

Let’s see this in action:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, QGSimulatedAnnealing, SimulatedAnnealing

# Create a simple 2-cluster graph for demonstration
demo_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[20, 20], p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]])
demo_graph.precomputing()
demo_points = demo_graph.sample_points(50)

print(f"Demo graph: {demo_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {len(demo_points)} points")

Demo graph: 40 nodes, 50 points

docs.title: Temperature Control
docs.text:
The balance between exploration and exploitation is controlled by temperature:

  • High temperature → More Brownian motion (exploration)
  • Low temperature → More drift (exploitation)
# High temperature (more exploration)
high_temp_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, lambda_param=3.0, beta=0.5)
high_temp_centers = high_temp_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

# Low temperature (more exploitation)
low_temp_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, lambda_param=0.5, beta=2.0)
low_temp_centers = low_temp_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

print(f"High temperature centers: {high_temp_centers}")
print(f"Low temperature centers: {low_temp_centers}")

High temperature centers: [20, 33]
Low temperature centers: [13, 33]

Temperature decreases over time using an inhomogeneous Poisson process.

docs.title: Version 1 (v1) - Interleaved
docs.text: For each iteration: brownian_motion() → drift_step()

docs.title: Version 2 (v2) - Sequential
docs.text:
First half iterations: pure exploration, second half: pure exploitation

# Compare algorithm versions
v1_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
v1_centers = v1_sa.run(algorithm_version="v1", initialization="kpp")

v2_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)  
v2_centers = v2_sa.run(algorithm_version="v2", initialization="kpp")

print(f"Version 1 found {len(v1_centers)} centers")
print(f"Version 2 found {len(v2_centers)} centers")

Version 1 found 2 centers
Version 2 found 2 centers

Choose based on your problem: - v1: Balanced for most cases - v2: Better when you need distinct exploration/exploitation phases

docs.title: Robustification
docs.text:
Instead of using just the final result, kmeanssa-ng averages the last few iterations:

# Compare different robustification levels
robust_levels = [0.05, 0.1, 0.2]  # 5%, 10%, 20%

print("Robustification comparison:")
for prop in robust_levels:
    sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
    centers = sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=prop)
    print(f"  {prop*100:2.0f}% robustification: {centers}")

Robustification comparison:
   5% robustification: [19, 34]
  10% robustification: [1, 16]
  20% robustification: [36, 24]

Benefits: - Reduces sensitivity to random fluctuations - More stable and reproducible results - Better convergence in noisy scenarios

docs.title: Algorithm Parameters
docs.text:
• k: Number of clusters (like standard k-means)
  • lambda_param: Temperature parameter (higher = more exploration)
  • beta: Drift strength (higher = stronger attraction to data)
  • step_size: Random walk step size

docs.title: Tuning Guidelines
docs.text:

docs.title: Distance Computation
docs.text:
Quantum graphs use geodesic distance - the shortest path along the graph:

# Demonstrate distance calculation
# Create two points on different edges
point1 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=(0, 1), position=0.2)
point2 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=(5, 8), position=0.7)

distance = demo_graph.distance(point1, point2)
print(f"Geodesic distance between points on different edges: {distance:.3f}")

# Compare with points on same edge
point3 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=(0, 1), position=0.8)
same_edge_distance = demo_graph.distance(point1, point3)
print(f"Distance between points on same edge: {same_edge_distance:.3f}")

Geodesic distance between points on different edges: 1.500
Distance between points on same edge: 0.600

Performance tip: Always call graph.precomputing() to cache shortest paths between all node pairs.

docs.text:
The QGSimulatedAnnealing class provides quantum graph specific methods:

# Quantum graph specific clustering
qg_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)

# Get results as node IDs (more interpretable)

print(f"Centers near nodes: {node_centers}")

mean_node = mean_sa.run_for_mean(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Overall mean near node: {mean_node}")

# Compare with exact positions

exact_centers = regular_sa.run(initialization="kpp")

docs.title: Custom Metric Spaces
docs.text: While quantum graphs are powerful, you can extend kmeanssa-ng to work with any metric space by implementing the abstract base classes. This guide shows you how to create custom metric spaces for specialized clustering applications.

docs.title: Understanding the Architecture
docs.text:
The library uses a three-layer abstraction pattern:

  1. Point - Immutable elements in your metric space
  2. Center - Movable points that serve as cluster centers
  3. Space - The metric space containing points and distance computation
from kmeanssa_ng.core.abstract import Point, Center, Space
from kmeanssa_ng import SimulatedAnnealing
import numpy as np
import math

print("Abstract base classes imported successfully!")

Abstract base classes imported successfully!

docs.title: Creating a Simple 1D Metric Space
docs.text:
Let’s start with a basic example - clustering on a line (1D space):

# 1D Point implementation
class LinePoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, x: float):
        self._space = space
        self.x = x

    @property 
    def space(self):
        return self._space

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"LinePoint(x={self.x:.2f})"

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return isinstance(other, LinePoint) and abs(self.x - other.x) < 1e-10

    def __hash__(self):
        return hash(round(self.x, 10))

# 1D Center implementation  
class LineCenter(LinePoint, Center):
    def __init__(self, space, x: float):
        super().__init__(space, x)

    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random walk along the line."""
        step = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))
        self.x += step

        # Keep within space bounds
        self.x = max(self._space.min_x, min(self._space.max_x, self.x))

    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward target point along the line."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, LinePoint):
            raise ValueError("Target must be a LinePoint")

        direction = target_point.x - self.x
        self.x += direction * prop_to_travel

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"LineCenter(x={self.x:.2f})"

# 1D Space implementation
class LineSpace(Space):
    def __init__(self, min_x: float = 0.0, max_x: float = 10.0):
        self.min_x = min_x
        self.max_x = max_x

    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Euclidean distance on the line."""
        if not isinstance(p1, LinePoint) or not isinstance(p2, LinePoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be LinePoint instances")
        return abs(p1.x - p2.x)

    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample random points uniformly on the line."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            points.append(LinePoint(self, x))
        return points

    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample random centers uniformly on the line."""
        centers = []
        for _ in range(k):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            centers.append(LineCenter(self, x))
        return centers

    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for 1D space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback for 1D

    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        """Not needed for basic usage."""
        pass

    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        """Calculate clustering energy (not used in this example)."""
        return 0.0

    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

# Test the 1D space
line_space = LineSpace(0, 10)
points_1d = line_space.sample_points(20)
centers_1d = line_space.sample_centers(3)

print(f"Sample 1D points: {points_1d[:5]}")
print(f"Sample 1D centers: {centers_1d}")
print(f"Distance between first two points: {line_space.distance(points_1d[0], points_1d[1]):.2f}")

Sample 1D points: [LinePoint(x=3.98), LinePoint(x=8.57), LinePoint(x=6.07), LinePoint(x=4.47), LinePoint(x=6.37)]
Sample 1D centers: [LineCenter(x=7.41), LineCenter(x=4.09), LineCenter(x=3.84)]
Distance between first two points: 4.59

docs.title: Advanced Example: 2D Euclidean Space
docs.text:
Let’s create a more sophisticated 2D space with custom distance metrics:

# 2D Point implementation
class Point2D(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, x: float, y: float):
        self._space = space
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

    @property
    def space(self):
        return self._space

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Point2D({self.x:.2f}, {self.y:.2f})"

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return (isinstance(other, Point2D) and 
                abs(self.x - other.x) < 1e-10 and 
                abs(self.y - other.y) < 1e-10)

    def __hash__(self):
        return hash((round(self.x, 10), round(self.y, 10)))

# 2D Center implementation
class Center2D(Point2D, Center):
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """2D random walk."""
        step_x = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))
        step_y = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))

        self.x += step_x
        self.y += step_y

        # Keep within bounds
        self.x = max(self._space.min_x, min(self._space.max_x, self.x))
        self.y = max(self._space.min_y, min(self._space.max_y, self.y))

    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward target in 2D."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, Point2D):
            raise ValueError("Target must be a Point2D")

        dx = target_point.x - self.x
        dy = target_point.y - self.y

        self.x += dx * prop_to_travel
        self.y += dy * prop_to_travel

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Center2D({self.x:.2f}, {self.y:.2f})"

# 2D Space with custom distance metrics
class Space2D(Space):
    def __init__(self, min_x=0, max_x=10, min_y=0, max_y=10, metric="euclidean"):
        self.min_x, self.max_x = min_x, max_x
        self.min_y, self.max_y = min_y, max_y
        self.metric = metric

    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Multiple distance metrics."""
        if not isinstance(p1, Point2D) or not isinstance(p2, Point2D):
            raise ValueError("Points must be Point2D instances")

        dx = p1.x - p2.x
        dy = p1.y - p2.y

        if self.metric == "euclidean":
            return math.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy)
        elif self.metric == "manhattan": 
            return abs(dx) + abs(dy)
        elif self.metric == "chebyshev":
            return max(abs(dx), abs(dy))
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown metric: {self.metric}")

    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample random 2D points."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            y = np.random.uniform(self.min_y, self.max_y)
            points.append(Point2D(self, x, y))
        return points

    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample random 2D centers."""
        centers = []
        for _ in range(k):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            y = np.random.uniform(self.min_y, self.max_y)
            centers.append(Center2D(self, x, y))
        return centers

    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for 2D space."""
        if k <= 0:
            return []

        # Start with a random center
        centers = [Center2D(self, 
                          np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x),
                          np.random.uniform(self.min_y, self.max_y))]

        # Add remaining centers using k-means++ logic
        for _ in range(k - 1):
            # Sample many candidates and pick the one farthest from existing centers
            best_distance = 0
            best_center = None

            for _ in range(50):  # Try 50 candidates
                candidate = Center2D(self,
                                   np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x),
                                   np.random.uniform(self.min_y, self.max_y))

                min_dist_to_existing = min(self.distance(candidate, center) for center in centers)

                if min_dist_to_existing > best_distance:
                    best_distance = min_dist_to_existing
                    best_center = candidate

            if best_center is not None:
                centers.append(best_center)

        return centers

    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        pass

    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        return 0.0

    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

# Test different distance metrics
metrics = ["euclidean", "manhattan", "chebyshev"]
results = {}

for metric in metrics:
    space_2d = Space2D(0, 10, 0, 10, metric=metric)
    points_2d = space_2d.sample_points(50)

    sa_2d = SimulatedAnnealing(points_2d, k=3)
    centers_2d = sa_2d.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.05)
    energy_2d = sa_2d.calculate_energy(centers_2d, points_2d)

    results[metric] = energy_2d
    print(f"{metric.capitalize()} metric - Final energy: {energy_2d:.3f}")

print(f"\nBest metric: {min(results, key=results.get)} (lowest energy)")

Euclidean metric - Final energy: 10.162
Manhattan metric - Final energy: 15.130
Chebyshev metric - Final energy: 11.492

Best metric: euclidean (lowest energy)

docs.title: Specialized Example: Circular Space
docs.text:
Here’s an example of clustering on a circle, where distances wrap around:

# Circular space (angles on a circle)
class CircularPoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, angle: float):
        self._space = space  
        self.angle = angle % (2 * math.pi)  # Normalize to [0, 2π)

    @property
    def space(self):
        return self._space

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"CircularPoint(θ={self.angle:.2f})"

class CircularCenter(CircularPoint, Center):
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random walk on the circle."""
        step = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))
        self.angle = (self.angle + step) % (2 * math.pi)

    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward target on circle (shortest path)."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, CircularPoint):
            raise ValueError("Target must be CircularPoint")

        # Calculate shortest angular distance
        diff = target_point.angle - self.angle

        # Wrap to [-π, π] for shortest path
        while diff > math.pi:
            diff -= 2 * math.pi
        while diff < -math.pi:
            diff += 2 * math.pi

        self.angle = (self.angle + diff * prop_to_travel) % (2 * math.pi)

class CircularSpace(Space):
    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Angular distance on circle."""
        if not isinstance(p1, CircularPoint) or not isinstance(p2, CircularPoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be CircularPoint instances")

        diff = abs(p1.angle - p2.angle)
        return min(diff, 2 * math.pi - diff)  # Shortest arc

    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample random angles."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            angle = np.random.uniform(0, 2 * math.pi)
            points.append(CircularPoint(self, angle))
        return points

    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample random center angles."""
        centers = []
        for _ in range(k):
            angle = np.random.uniform(0, 2 * math.pi)
            centers.append(CircularCenter(self, angle))
        return centers

    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for circular space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback

    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        pass

    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        return 0.0

    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

# Test circular clustering
circular_space = CircularSpace()

# Create clustered data (3 groups around the circle)
clustered_points = []
cluster_centers = [0.5, 2.5, 4.5]  # Roughly evenly spaced

for center in cluster_centers:
    for _ in range(15):
        # Add some noise around each cluster center
        noise = np.random.normal(0, 0.3)
        angle = center + noise
        clustered_points.append(CircularPoint(circular_space, angle))

print(f"Created {len(clustered_points)} points in 3 circular clusters")

# Run clustering
sa_circular = SimulatedAnnealing(clustered_points, k=3)
circular_centers = sa_circular.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
circular_energy = sa_circular.calculate_energy(circular_centers, clustered_points)

print(f"Circular clustering energy: {circular_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Found centers at angles: {[f'{c.angle:.2f}' for c in circular_centers]}")

# Verify clustering makes sense
for i, center in enumerate(circular_centers):
    nearby_points = [p for p in clustered_points if circular_space.distance(p, center) < 1.0]
    print(f"Cluster {i}: {len(nearby_points)} points near angle {center.angle:.2f}")

Created 45 points in 3 circular clusters
Circular clustering energy: 0.732
Found centers at angles: ['5.45', '3.81', '2.68']
Cluster 0: 11 points near angle 5.45
Cluster 1: 13 points near angle 3.81
Cluster 2: 15 points near angle 2.68

docs.title: Integration with k-means++ Initialization
docs.text:
For optimal results, implement k-means++ initialization in your Space class:

# Enhanced 2D space with proper k-means++
class Enhanced2DSpace(Space2D):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._cached_points = None

    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Proper k-means++ initialization."""
        # Generate a reference set of points for initialization
        if self._cached_points is None:
            self._cached_points = self.sample_points(200)  # Cache for efficiency

        if k <= 0:
            return []

        centers = []

        # Choose first center randomly
        first_point = np.random.choice(self._cached_points)
        centers.append(Center2D(self, first_point.x, first_point.y))

        # Choose remaining centers with probability proportional to squared distance
        for _ in range(k - 1):
            distances = []

            for point in self._cached_points:
                min_dist = min(self.distance(point, center) for center in centers)
                distances.append(min_dist * min_dist)  # Squared distance

            # Weighted random selection
            total = sum(distances)
            if total > 0:
                probs = [d / total for d in distances]
                chosen_idx = np.random.choice(len(self._cached_points), p=probs)
                chosen_point = self._cached_points[chosen_idx]
                centers.append(Center2D(self, chosen_point.x, chosen_point.y))

        return centers

# Test enhanced initialization
enhanced_space = Enhanced2DSpace(0, 10, 0, 10, "euclidean")
points_enhanced = enhanced_space.sample_points(100)

# Compare random vs k-means++ initialization
sa_random = SimulatedAnnealing(points_enhanced, k=4)
centers_random = sa_random.run(initialization="random", robust_prop=0.05)
energy_random = sa_random.calculate_energy(centers_random, points_enhanced)

sa_kpp = SimulatedAnnealing(points_enhanced, k=4)
centers_kpp = sa_kpp.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.05)  
energy_kpp = sa_kpp.calculate_energy(centers_kpp, points_enhanced)

print(f"Random initialization energy: {energy_random:.3f}")
print(f"k-means++ initialization energy: {energy_kpp:.3f}")
print(f"Improvement: {energy_random - energy_kpp:.3f}")

Random initialization energy: 11.592
k-means++ initialization energy: 7.473
Improvement: 4.119

docs.title: Batch Distance Computation
docs.text:
For performance, implement batch distance operations:

class OptimizedSpace2D(Enhanced2DSpace):
    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers efficiently."""
        if not isinstance(point, Point2D):
            raise ValueError("Point must be Point2D")

        distances = []
        for center in centers:
            if isinstance(center, Point2D):
                distances.append(self.distance(point, center))
            else:
                distances.append(float('inf'))

        return distances

# Test batch computation
opt_space = OptimizedSpace2D()
test_centers = opt_space.sample_centers(5)
test_point = Point2D(opt_space, 5.0, 5.0)

batch_distances = opt_space.batch_distances_from_centers(test_centers, test_point)
individual_distances = [opt_space.distance(test_point, c) for c in test_centers]

print(f"Batch distances: {[f'{d:.2f}' for d in batch_distances]}")
print(f"Individual distances: {[f'{d:.2f}' for d in individual_distances]}")
print(f"Results match: {np.allclose(batch_distances, individual_distances)}")

docs.title: Custom Similarity Spaces
docs.text:
Create spaces based on similarity rather than distance:

# Similarity-based space (higher values = more similar)
class SimilaritySpace(Space):
    def __init__(self, similarity_matrix: np.ndarray, labels: list = None):
        self.similarity_matrix = similarity_matrix
        self.n_objects = similarity_matrix.shape[0]
        self.labels = labels or list(range(self.n_objects))

        # Convert similarities to distances (distance = max_sim - similarity)
        self.max_sim = np.max(similarity_matrix)
        self.distance_matrix = self.max_sim - similarity_matrix

    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Distance derived from similarity."""
        if not isinstance(p1, SimilarityPoint) or not isinstance(p2, SimilarityPoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be SimilarityPoint instances")
        return self.distance_matrix[p1.object_id, p2.object_id]

    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample object IDs."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            obj_id = np.random.randint(0, self.n_objects)
            points.append(SimilarityPoint(self, obj_id))
        return points

    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample object IDs as centers."""
        centers = []
        chosen_ids = np.random.choice(self.n_objects, size=min(k, self.n_objects), replace=False)
        for obj_id in chosen_ids:
            centers.append(SimilarityCenter(self, obj_id))
        return centers

    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for similarity space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback

    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        pass

    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        return 0.0

    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

class SimilarityPoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, object_id: int):
        self._space = space
        self.object_id = object_id

    @property
    def space(self):
        return self._space

    def __repr__(self):
        label = self._space.labels[self.object_id] if self._space.labels else self.object_id
        return f"SimilarityPoint({label})"

class SimilarityCenter(SimilarityPoint, Center):
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random jump to nearby object."""
        # Find most similar objects and randomly jump to one
        similarities = self._space.similarity_matrix[self.object_id]
        top_similar = np.argsort(similarities)[-5:]  # Top 5 most similar
        self.object_id = np.random.choice(top_similar)

    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward most similar object to target."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, SimilarityPoint):
            return

        # With some probability, jump to target or similar object
        if np.random.random() < prop_to_travel:
            target_similarities = self._space.similarity_matrix[target_point.object_id]
            most_similar = np.argmax(target_similarities)
            self.object_id = most_similar

# Test similarity space
similarity_data = np.array([
    [1.0, 0.9, 0.1, 0.2, 0.1],
    [0.9, 1.0, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2], 
    [0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 0.8, 0.9],
    [0.2, 0.1, 0.8, 1.0, 0.7],
    [0.1, 0.2, 0.9, 0.7, 1.0]
])

labels = ['Doc1', 'Doc2', 'Doc3', 'Doc4', 'Doc5']
sim_space = SimilaritySpace(similarity_data, labels)

# Generate points (each object appears multiple times)
sim_points = []
for obj_id in range(5):
    for _ in range(4):  # 4 copies of each object
        sim_points.append(SimilarityPoint(sim_space, obj_id))

print(f"Similarity points: {sim_points[:10]}")

# Cluster in similarity space
sa_sim = SimulatedAnnealing(sim_points, k=2)
sim_centers = sa_sim.run(robust_prop=0.1)
sim_energy = sa_sim.calculate_energy(sim_centers, sim_points)

print(f"Similarity clustering energy: {sim_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Final centers: {sim_centers}")

Similarity points: [SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc3), SimilarityPoint(Doc3)]
Similarity clustering energy: 0.384
Final centers: [SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc1)]

docs.title: Implementation Guidelines
docs.text:
# Performance and correctness checklist
def validate_custom_space(space_class, n_test_points=50):
    """Validate a custom metric space implementation."""
    print(f"Validating {space_class.__name__}...")

    try:
        # Test instantiation
        space = space_class()
        print("✓ Space instantiation successful")

        # Test point sampling
        points = space.sample_points(n_test_points)
        print(f"✓ Point sampling: {len(points)} points generated")

        # Test center sampling  
        centers = space.sample_centers(3)
        print(f"✓ Center sampling: {len(centers)} centers generated")

        # Test distance computation
        if len(points) >= 2:
            dist = space.distance(points[0], points[1])
            print(f"✓ Distance computation: d = {dist:.3f}")

            # Check metric properties
            # 1. Non-negativity
            assert dist >= 0, "Distance must be non-negative"

            # 2. Identity of indiscernibles  
            self_dist = space.distance(points[0], points[0])
            assert abs(self_dist) < 1e-10, "Distance to self must be zero"

            # 3. Symmetry
            reverse_dist = space.distance(points[1], points[0])
            assert abs(dist - reverse_dist) < 1e-10, "Distance must be symmetric"
            print("✓ Metric properties validated")

        # Test clustering integration
        from kmeanssa_ng import SimulatedAnnealing
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points[:20], k=2)
        result_centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.05)
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(result_centers, points[:20])
        print(f"✓ Clustering integration: final energy = {energy:.3f}")

        print(f"✅ {space_class.__name__} validation successful!\n")
        return True

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ {space_class.__name__} validation failed: {e}\n")
        return False

# Test our implementations
validate_custom_space(LineSpace)
validate_custom_space(lambda: Space2D(metric="euclidean"))
validate_custom_space(CircularSpace)

docs.title: Graph Generators
docs.text: Graph generators are essential for testing, benchmarking, and experimenting with clustering algorithms. This guide covers all the graph generation functions in kmeanssa-ng, from simple test cases to complex stochastic models.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The library provides several types of graph generators:

  • Simple graphs: Two-cluster test cases for development
  • Stochastic Block Models (SBM): Multi-cluster graphs with controllable structure
  • NetworkX converters: Transform existing graphs to quantum graphs
  • Complete graphs: Dense graphs from similarity matrices
import numpy as np
import networkx as nx
from kmeanssa_ng import (
    generate_simple_graph, 
    generate_sbm, 
    generate_random_sbm,
    as_quantum_graph,
    complete_quantum_graph
)

print("Graph generators loaded successfully!")

Graph generators loaded successfully!

docs.title: Clustering Performance
docs.text:
# Test clustering performance on different graph types
from kmeanssa_ng import QGSimulatedAnnealing

test_graphs = [
    ("Simple 2-cluster", generate_simple_graph(n_a=8, n_aa=4, bridge_length=5.0)),
    ("SBM 2-block", generate_sbm(sizes=[40, 40], p=[[0.9, 0.05], [0.05, 0.9]])),
    ("SBM 3-block", generate_sbm(sizes=[30, 30, 30], p=[[0.8, 0.1, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 0.8]]))
]

print("Clustering performance comparison:")
print(f"{'Graph Type':<15} {'k':<3} {'Energy':<8} {'Runtime'}")
print("-" * 40)

import time

for name, graph in test_graphs:
    points = graph.sample_points(80)
    k = 2 if "2" in name else 3

    start_time = time.time()
    sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
    centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.05, initialization="kpp") 
    runtime = time.time() - start_time

# Validate generated graphs
def validate_quantum_graph(graph, name):
    """Validate that a quantum graph is properly constructed."""
    issues = []

    # Check connectivity
    if not nx.is_connected(graph):
        issues.append("Graph not connected")

    # Check edge lengths
    try:
        graph.validate_edge_lengths()
    except ValueError as e:
        issues.append(f"Edge validation failed: {e}")

    # Check node weights
    node_weights = nx.get_node_attributes(graph, 'weight')
    if not all(w > 0 for w in node_weights.values()):
        issues.append("Some node weights are not positive")

    # Check if precomputing works
    try:
        graph.precomputing() 
    except Exception as e:
        issues.append(f"Precomputing failed: {e}")

    if issues:
        print(f"⚠️  {name}: {', '.join(issues)}")
    else:
        print(f"✅ {name}: Valid quantum graph")

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, SimulatedAnnealing, QGSimulatedAnnealing
import time
import numpy as np
import networkx as nx
import psutil
import os

def profile_memory_usage():
    """Get current memory usage of the process."""
    process = psutil.Process(os.getpid())
    return process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # MB

def benchmark_clustering(graph, n_points, k, description):
    """Benchmark clustering performance with detailed metrics."""

    print(f"=== {description} ===")

    # Memory before
    mem_before = profile_memory_usage()

    # Sample points
    start_time = time.time()
    points = graph.sample_points(n_points)
    sampling_time = time.time() - start_time
    mem_after_sampling = profile_memory_usage()

    # Run clustering
    start_time = time.time()
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
    centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
    clustering_time = time.time() - start_time
    mem_after_clustering = profile_memory_usage()

    # Calculate energy
    start_time = time.time()
    energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
    energy_time = time.time() - start_time

    # Report results
    print(f"Graph: {len(graph.nodes())} nodes, {len(graph.edges())} edges")
    print(f"Points: {n_points}, Clusters: {k}")
    print(f"Timings:")
    print(f"  Sampling: {sampling_time:.3f}s")
    print(f"  Clustering: {clustering_time:.3f}s")
    print(f"  Energy calc: {energy_time:.3f}s")
    print(f"  Total: {sampling_time + clustering_time + energy_time:.3f}s")
    print(f"Memory usage:")
    print(f"  Initial: {mem_before:.1f} MB")
    print(f"  After sampling: {mem_after_sampling:.1f} MB (+{mem_after_sampling - mem_before:.1f})")
    print(f"  After clustering: {mem_after_clustering:.1f} MB (+{mem_after_clustering - mem_after_sampling:.1f})")
    print(f"Final energy: {energy:.3f}")
    print()

    return {
        'sampling_time': sampling_time,
        'clustering_time': clustering_time,
        'energy_time': energy_time,
        'total_time': sampling_time + clustering_time + energy_time,
        'memory_peak': mem_after_clustering,
        'energy': energy
    }

def optimize_graph_structure(graph, max_nodes=1000):
    """Optimize graph structure for better clustering performance."""

    original_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    original_edges = len(graph.edges())

    print(f"Original graph: {original_nodes} nodes, {original_edges} edges")

    # Strategy 1: Work with largest connected component
    if not nx.is_connected(graph):
        largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(graph), key=len)
        graph = graph.subgraph(largest_cc).copy()
        print(f"Extracted largest component: {len(graph.nodes())} nodes")

    # Strategy 2: Sample nodes if graph is too large
    if len(graph.nodes()) > max_nodes:
        # Sample nodes while preserving connectivity
        sampled_nodes = list(graph.nodes())[:max_nodes]
        graph = graph.subgraph(sampled_nodes).copy()

        # Ensure connectivity
        if not nx.is_connected(graph):
            largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(graph), key=len)
            graph = graph.subgraph(largest_cc).copy()

        print(f"Sampled to {len(graph.nodes())} nodes")

    # Strategy 3: Precompute distances
    graph.precomputing()
    print("Distance precomputation completed")

    # Strategy 4: Validate edge weights for sampling efficiency
    edge_lengths = [d['length'] for u, v, d in graph.edges(data=True)]
    if edge_lengths:
        avg_length = np.mean(edge_lengths)
        std_length = np.std(edge_lengths)
        print(f"Edge lengths: μ={avg_length:.3f}, σ={std_length:.3f}")

        # Normalize extreme lengths if needed
        if std_length > 10 * avg_length:
            print("Warning: High variance in edge lengths may affect performance")

    final_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    final_edges = len(graph.edges())
    reduction_factor = original_nodes / final_nodes if final_nodes > 0 else 1

    print(f"Final graph: {final_nodes} nodes, {final_edges} edges")
    print(f"Reduction factor: {reduction_factor:.2f}x")
    print()

    return graph

# Test graph optimization
print("=== Graph Optimization ===")
test_large_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[150, 150, 100], p=[[0.6, 0.1, 0.05], [0.1, 0.6, 0.1], [0.05, 0.1, 0.6]])
optimized_graph = optimize_graph_structure(test_large_graph, max_nodes=200)

# Compare performance before/after optimization
original_result = benchmark_clustering(test_large_graph, 150, 3, "Before Optimization")
optimized_result = benchmark_clustering(optimized_graph, 150, 3, "After Optimization")

speedup = original_result['total_time'] / optimized_result['total_time']
print(f"Optimization speedup: {speedup:.2f}x")

def get_fast_parameters(n_points, k, graph_density=0.1):
    """Get parameter settings optimized for speed."""

    base_params = {
        'lambda_param': 1.0,
        'beta': 1.0, 
        'step_size': 0.1
    }

    # Adjust for dataset size
    if n_points > 500:
        base_params['lambda_param'] = 2.0  # Higher exploration for large datasets
        base_params['step_size'] = 0.15    # Larger steps for faster convergence
    elif n_points < 100:
        base_params['beta'] = 2.0          # Faster convergence for small datasets
        base_params['step_size'] = 0.05    # Smaller steps for precision

    # Adjust for number of clusters
    if k > 5:
        base_params['lambda_param'] *= 1.5  # More exploration for many clusters

    # Adjust for graph density
    if graph_density > 0.2:  # Dense graph
        base_params['step_size'] *= 0.8    # Smaller steps in dense graphs
    elif graph_density < 0.05:  # Sparse graph
        base_params['step_size'] *= 1.3    # Larger steps in sparse graphs

    return base_params

def get_quality_parameters(n_points, k):
    """Get parameter settings optimized for clustering quality."""

    return {
        'lambda_param': 0.8,    # Moderate exploration
        'beta': 0.7,           # Slower convergence for better quality  
        'step_size': 0.05      # Smaller steps for precision
    }

def memory_efficient_clustering(graph, n_points, k, batch_size=100):
    """Perform clustering with memory optimization techniques."""

    print(f"=== Memory-Efficient Clustering ===")
    print(f"Processing {n_points} points in batches of {batch_size}")

    mem_start = profile_memory_usage()

    # Strategy 1: Batch processing of points
    all_points = []
    for batch_start in range(0, n_points, batch_size):
        batch_end = min(batch_start + batch_size, n_points)
        batch_points = graph.sample_points(batch_end - batch_start)
        all_points.extend(batch_points)

        if batch_start % (batch_size * 3) == 0:  # Report every 3 batches
            mem_current = profile_memory_usage()
            print(f"Batch {batch_start//batch_size + 1}: {mem_current:.1f} MB (+{mem_current - mem_start:.1f})")

    mem_after_sampling = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Sampling complete: {mem_after_sampling:.1f} MB")

    # Strategy 2: Use smaller robustification for memory efficiency
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(all_points, k=k, step_size=0.15)  # Larger steps = faster = less memory over time
    centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.05, initialization="kpp")  # Smaller robustification

    mem_after_clustering = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Clustering complete: {mem_after_clustering:.1f} MB")

    # Strategy 3: Incremental energy calculation
    total_energy = 0.0
    energy_batch_size = batch_size // 2

    for i in range(0, len(all_points), energy_batch_size):
        batch_points = all_points[i:i + energy_batch_size]
        batch_energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, batch_points)
        total_energy += batch_energy * len(batch_points)

    average_energy = total_energy / len(all_points)

    mem_final = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Final memory: {mem_final:.1f} MB")
    print(f"Peak memory usage: {max(mem_after_sampling, mem_after_clustering, mem_final):.1f} MB")
    print(f"Average energy: {average_energy:.3f}")

    return centers, average_energy

# Test memory-efficient approach
test_graph_large = generate_sbm(sizes=[80, 80, 60], p=[[0.7, 0.1, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 0.7]])

def parallel_initialization_comparison(points, k, n_trials=5):
    """Compare different initialization strategies for consistency and speed."""

    print(f"=== Initialization Strategy Comparison ===")
    print(f"Testing with {len(points)} points, k={k}, {n_trials} trials each")

    strategies = ['random', 'kpp']
    results = {}

    for strategy in strategies:
        energies = []
        times = []

        print(f"\nTesting {strategy} initialization:")

        for trial in range(n_trials):
            start_time = time.time()
            sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
            centers = sa.run(initialization=strategy, robust_prop=0.1)
            end_time = time.time()

            energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
            runtime = end_time - start_time

            energies.append(energy)
            times.append(runtime)

            if trial < 3:  # Show first few trials
                print(f"  Trial {trial + 1}: {runtime:.3f}s, energy={energy:.3f}")

        # Statistics
        mean_energy = np.mean(energies)
        std_energy = np.std(energies)
        mean_time = np.mean(times)
        std_time = np.std(times)

        results[strategy] = {
            'mean_energy': mean_energy,
            'std_energy': std_energy,
            'mean_time': mean_time,
            'std_time': std_time,
            'consistency': std_energy / mean_energy  # Lower is more consistent
        }

        print(f"  Average: {mean_time:.3f}±{std_time:.3f}s, energy={mean_energy:.3f}±{std_energy:.3f}")
        print(f"  Consistency score: {results[strategy]['consistency']:.4f}")

    # Recommendation
    if results['kpp']['consistency'] < results['random']['consistency']:
        print(f"\nRecommendation: Use k-means++ for better consistency")
    else:
        print(f"\nRecommendation: Random initialization is sufficient")

    if results['kpp']['mean_time'] > results['random']['mean_time'] * 1.5:
        print(f"Note: k-means++ is {results['kpp']['mean_time'] / results['random']['mean_time']:.1f}x slower")

    return results

def compare_algorithm_versions(points, k):
    """Compare algorithm versions for different scenarios."""

    print(f"=== Algorithm Version Comparison ===")
    print(f"Dataset: {len(points)} points, k={k}")

    versions = ['v1', 'v2']
    version_results = {}

    for version in versions:
        print(f"\nTesting algorithm {version}:")

        # Multiple runs for stability
        energies = []
        times = []

        for run in range(3):
            start_time = time.time()
            sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
            centers = sa.run(
                algorithm_version=version,
                robust_prop=0.1,
                initialization="kpp"
            )
            end_time = time.time()

            energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
            runtime = end_time - start_time

            energies.append(energy)
            times.append(runtime)

            print(f"  Run {run + 1}: {runtime:.3f}s, energy={energy:.3f}")

        mean_energy = np.mean(energies)
        mean_time = np.mean(times)

        version_results[version] = {
            'mean_energy': mean_energy,
            'mean_time': mean_time,
            'energies': energies,
            'times': times
        }

        print(f"  Average: {mean_time:.3f}s, energy={mean_energy:.3f}")

    # Analysis and recommendation
    v1_result = version_results['v1']
    v2_result = version_results['v2']

    speed_ratio = v1_result['mean_time'] / v2_result['mean_time']
    energy_ratio = v1_result['mean_energy'] / v2_result['mean_energy']

    print(f"\n=== Comparison Analysis ===")
    print(f"Speed: v1 vs v2 = {speed_ratio:.2f} (lower is faster)")
    print(f"Energy: v1 vs v2 = {energy_ratio:.3f} (lower is better)")

    if speed_ratio < 0.9:
        print("Recommendation: Use v1 (interleaved) for better speed")
    elif speed_ratio > 1.1:
        print("Recommendation: Use v2 (sequential) for better speed")
    else:
        print("Recommendation: Both versions have similar performance")

    if abs(energy_ratio - 1.0) > 0.05:
        better_quality = "v1" if energy_ratio < 1.0 else "v2"
        print(f"Note: {better_quality} provides better clustering quality")

    return version_results

# Compare algorithm versions

def create_performance_monitor():
    """Create a simple performance monitoring utility."""

    class PerformanceMonitor:
        def __init__(self):
            self.start_time = None
            self.start_memory = None
            self.checkpoints = []

        def start(self):
            self.start_time = time.time()
            self.start_memory = profile_memory_usage()
            self.checkpoints = []
            print(f"Performance monitoring started (Memory: {self.start_memory:.1f} MB)")

        def checkpoint(self, name):
            current_time = time.time()
            current_memory = profile_memory_usage()

            elapsed = current_time - self.start_time
            memory_delta = current_memory - self.start_memory

            self.checkpoints.append({
                'name': name,
                'elapsed': elapsed,
                'memory': current_memory,
                'memory_delta': memory_delta
            })

            print(f"Checkpoint '{name}': {elapsed:.3f}s, {current_memory:.1f} MB (+{memory_delta:+.1f})")

        def finish(self):
            if self.checkpoints:
                total_time = self.checkpoints[-1]['elapsed']
                peak_memory = max(cp['memory'] for cp in self.checkpoints)
                print(f"Monitoring complete: {total_time:.3f}s total, {peak_memory:.1f} MB peak")
            return self.checkpoints

    return PerformanceMonitor()

# Example usage of performance monitor
print("=== Performance Monitoring Example ===")

monitor = create_performance_monitor()
monitor.start()

# Simulate a clustering workflow
test_graph_monitor = generate_sbm(sizes=[60, 60], p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]])
monitor.checkpoint("Graph created")

test_graph_monitor.precomputing()
monitor.checkpoint("Precomputation done")

test_points_monitor = test_graph_monitor.sample_points(150)
monitor.checkpoint("Points sampled")

sa_monitor = SimulatedAnnealing(test_points_monitor, k=2)
monitor.checkpoint("SA initialized")

centers_monitor = sa_monitor.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
monitor.checkpoint("Clustering complete")

energy_monitor = sa_monitor.calculate_energy(centers_monitor, test_points_monitor)
monitor.checkpoint("Energy calculated")

checkpoints = monitor.finish()

# Bottleneck analysis
print(f"\nBottleneck analysis:")
for i in range(1, len(checkpoints)):
    step_time = checkpoints[i]['elapsed'] - checkpoints[i-1]['elapsed']
    step_memory = checkpoints[i]['memory_delta'] - checkpoints[i-1]['memory_delta']
    print(f"{checkpoints[i-1]['name']} → {checkpoints[i]['name']}: {step_time:.3f}s, {step_memory:+.1f} MB")
